title: Shyam Sunder vs Smt. Pooja Verma on 24 September, 2013
body: Shyam Sunder vs Smt. Pooja Verma on 24 September, 2013 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma IN THE COURT OF MS. SHREYA ARORA MEHTA, CIVIL JUDGE­1, ROOM NO. 3, SAKET COURTS, SOUTH DISTRICT, NEW DELHI In the matter of : Suit No.539/11 Case ID No.02406C0271562011 Shyam Sunder s/o Late Sh.Hans Raj r/o H.P.T. 17 P & T Quarters, Sarojini Nagar, New Delhi. ....... Plaintiffs Versus Smt. Pooja Verma w/o Sh. Nawal Kishore Verma r/o B 96, Upper Ground Floor, Jawahar Park, Khanpur, Devali, New Delhi - 110062. ...... Defendant Date of Institution : 20.10.2011 Date of reserving the Judgment : 11.09.2013 Date of pronouncement : 24.09.2013 Decision : Decreed qua the relief of possession SUIT FOR POSSESSION, MENSE PROFITS AND DAMAGES JUDGMENT 1. This order shall dispose of an application under Order XII Rule 6 of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908 filed on behalf of the Suit No.539/11 Page 1 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma plaintiff for passing of decree on the admission of the defendant. The plaintiff has averred in the application that defendant Ms. Pooja Verma filed a suit for permanent injunction no. CS 120/10 and during the pendency of the suit the plaintiff herein served a legal notice of increase of rent by 10% which was paid by the defendant and accepted by the plaintiff. A notice for the vacation of the premises was served on the defendant and the present suit for possession, mesne profits and damages was filed. It is further averred that the defendant has filed the written statement and made a false story however she has admitted the tenancy and also admitted that she has paid Rs.77,000/­ as rent and accepted that she is the tenant under the plaintiff. The defendant has also admitted the written agreement and the signature there upon. 2. On the day when the present application was filed this Court examined the defendant and her husband under Order X of Code of Civil Procedure , 1908. In the statement recorded in the Court the defendant admitted the landlord tenant relationship. The defendant and her husband admitted that they have been depositing the rent of the suit property to the plaintiff since 2008. It is pertinent to mention that firstly the statement was made by the defendant and she deposed that rent @ Rs.3500/­ is Suit No.539/11 Page 2 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma payable to the plaintiff. Subsequently, the husband of the defendant deposed that after the receipt of the notice as to the enhancement of rent Rs.3850/­ is being payable as rent to the plaintiff herein. 3. Thereafter, pleadings were examined for the purposes of pronouncement of judgment on admissions, and the suit was then decreed for the relief of possession in respect of suit premises bearing no. B­96, Upper Ground Floor, Jawahar Park, Khanpur Devali, New Delhi­62 vide judgment / decree dated 09.08.12. 4. After the aforesaid judgment on admissions, the defendant herein filed an appeal before the court of Mr.Sandeep Yadav, Ld.ADJ­06 (South), taking the plea that he was not given an opportunity to file reply to the application under Order XII Rule 6 of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908. The matter was remanded back to this Court vide order dated 28.02.13 for giving an opportunity to the defendant to file reply to the present application. 5. The defendant has filed reply to the application under Order XII Rule 6 of Code of Civil Procedure , 1908 on 18.03.13. The plaintiff has also filed rejoinder to the reply to the application under Order XII Rule 6 of Code of Civil Procedure , 1908. Suit No.539/11 Page 3 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma 6. In the reply, the defendant has stated that this Court does not have jurisdiction since the defendant denies that she has paid increased rent by 10% to the plaintiff. It is stated that in the suit no. 220/10 filed by the defendant against the plaintiff herein vide order dated 01.03.11 the monthly rent was settled at Rs.3500/­ which was accepted by both the parties and the defendant paid the arrears of the rent to the plaintiff at the aforesaid settled rate of rent. It is stated that since order in that suit was in force at the time when the notice of the enhancement was served upon by the plaintiff it was not maintainable and after the disposal of the said suit there is no further notice for enhancement of the rent by the plaintiff upon the defendant. Further the defendant submits that they deny the service of the notice for vacation of the suit premises. 7. In the rejoinder to the aforesaid reply filed on behalf of the defendant, the plaintiff submits that the plaintiff has laid hand on the postal certificate issued by the Department of Posts which proves that two money ordes were sent by the defendant of Rs.3850/­ which fact has been admitted by the husband of the defendant in the Court. It is further stated that in the paragraph 6 of the plaint it has been mentioned that a notice under Section 6 A of the Delhi Rent Control, 1958 was served on the defendant Suit No.539/11 Page 4 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma and she accepted to increase the monthly rent from 10.07.11 at Rs.3850/­. In the written statement in reply on merits, the defendant has averred that the contents of paragraph 6 of the plaint is a matter of record. It is stated that similarly with paragraph 7 of the plaint wherein it is stated that the defendant had admitted that she is a tenant vide Lease Agreement dated 10.07.08 and also admitted the factum of increase of rent from 10.07.11 @ Rs.3850/­ which she has started paying. To this the defendant has averred in the written statement that it is a matter of record. It is thus stated that from the aforesaid reply to the paragraphs 6 and 7 of the plaint, there is a clear cut admission on the part of the defendant that the rent is Rs.3850/­ and not Rs.3500/­ p.m. 8. It is further stated that the arrears of rent were only paid because at that time the rent was not enhanced. After the enhancement of rent from the month of July/August, 2011, the rent has been paid @ Rs.3850/­ upto July, 2011 and August, 2011 and after that no amount has been paid by the defendant. 9. I have heard the counsel for the plaintiff and have perused the record. It is pertinent to mention that various adjournments were sought by the defendant to put forth her arguments on the present application under Order XII Rule 6 of Code of Civil Suit No.539/11 Page 5 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma Procedure, 1908. On 11.09.09, noting down the said conduct of the defendant on seeking adjournment on one ground or the other this Court proceeded to hear the arguments on behalf of the plaintiff with liberty to the defendant to file written submissions within seven working days. It is to be noted that on the same day, after lunch the counsel for the Defendant appeared and himself submitted before the Court that he will be filing the written submissions. Despite the submission of the counsel for the defendant, no written submissions have been filed till date. 10.The plaintiff has filed the present suit for possession of the ground floor of the property bearing no. B­96, Upper Ground Floor, Jawahar Park, Khanpur Devali, New Delhi­62 as well as for recovery of mesne profits and damages alongwith costs of the suit. The case of the plaintiff is that the suit property was let out to the defendant herein vide Lease Deed dated 10.07.08. The monthly rent as per the Lease Deed was settled @ Rs.3500/­ inclusive of the water and electricity charges. It is averred that the notice dated 10.06.11 under Section 6 A of the Delhi Rent Control, 1958 was served on the defendant whereby the monthly rent of the suit premises was enhanced by 10% from 10.07.11 @ Rs.3850/­. It is further stated that after the enhancement of rent, Suit No.539/11 Page 6 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma notice dated 26.07.11 was also served on the defendant terminating the tenancy. It is further averred that after the notice dated 26.07.11 another notice dated 22.09.11 was served upon the defendant for payment of arrears of rent, water and electricity charges till handing over the possession. It is stated that after the after the services of the notices dated 26.07.11 and 22.09.11, the defendant is in an unauthorized occupancy of the suit premises. Vide notice dated 22.09.11, the tenancy of the defendant was duly terminated after expiry of 15 days period. 11.Per contra, it is the case of the defendant that the sum of Rs. 3,00,000/­ as loan was given to the plaintiff by the defendant which was not endorsed by the plaintiff and the present suit was filed by the plaintiff to get the defendant vacate the premises without paying the aforesaid amount. 12.It is settled position of law that the admissions contemplated by Order XII Rule 6 of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908 be expressed or implied in the proceedings. It has been held in catena of judicial pronouncements that in a suit for possession filed by the landlord against the tenant, the following requirements have to be established for attracting the provisions of Order XII Rule 6 of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908: (i) There is a relationship of landlord and tenant between the parties; (ii) Suit No.539/11 Page 7 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma The rate of rent is more than Rs.3500/­pm; (iii) The tenancy is duly terminated by service of legal notice. 13.From the aforesaid pleadings of the parties it is clear that the landlord­tenant relationship is not in dispute between the parties. 14.The second aspect as to the rate of rent requires consideration in the present matter. The plea of the defendant is mainly that this Court does not have the jurisdiction to deal with the matter since the rate of rent between the parties is Rs.3500/­pm. 15.In the examination under Order X of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908 both the defendant and her husband admitted the landlord tenant relationship and that they have been depositing rent of the suit property to the plaintiff since 2008. Firstly, the statement was made by the defendant and she deposed that rent @ Rs.3500/­ is paid and subsequently the husband of the defendant deposed that after the receipt of notice as to the enhancement of rent Rs.3850/­ is being payable as rent to the plaintiff herein. In the notice dated 22.09.11 under section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act, the plaintiff has clearly stated therein, "that you remitted a sum of RS.7700/­ on 17.07.11 as arrears of rent for the month of July and August, 2011. The said money order was accepted by my client and the notice dated Suit No.539/11 Page 8 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma 22.09.11 stood waived". 16.The original postal receipts corroborating its dispatch are on record. It is not the case of the defendant that the address on which the said notice was sent was not the address of the defendant. As per the catena of judgment of the superior Courts, it has been categorically held that if the legal notice is duly supported by its original postal record there is nothing left for the said notice to be proved at trial. From the aforesaid notice of termination it is clear that defendant had sent the rent @ Rs.3850/­pm. Further, the plaintiff has annexed with the rejoinder to the reply of the defendant to the application under Order XII Rule 6 of the Code of Civil Procedure the copy of the money order received under RTI Act to establish that the money order of Rs.3850/­ was sent by the defendant herein. 17.In any case, after the service of notice for enhancement of rent dated 10.06.11 under Section 6 A of Delhi Rent Control Act, 1958 the legally payable rent of the suit premises is Rs.3850/­ from 10.07.11. Therefore, with the service of the said notice the rent payable to the plaintiff is Rs.3850/­ p.m. Thus as on the date of termination of the tenancy since the rate of rent was enhanced and was Rs.3850/­ it goes outside the purview of the Delhi Rent Control Act , 1958 and this Court has jurisdiction to deal with the Suit No.539/11 Page 9 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma present matter. 18.The termination of the tenancy is substantiated by the legal notice dated 22.09.11. Original postal receipts corroborating its dispatch are on record. Nothing is left to prove the termination notice at trial if the original postal receipts are on record. Even otherwise it has been held in Jeevan Diesels and Electricals Limited v. Jasbir Singh Chadha (HUF )(2001)183 DLT 712 that the tenancy would stands terminated under the general law on filing of a suit for eviction. Accordingly, even assuming the notice of termination of tenancy was not served upon the defendant though it has been served as held by me above, the tenancy would stand terminated on filing of the present suit. 19.Hence after the termination of the tenancy the defendant is liable to vacate the suit premises and the plaintiff is entitled to recover the possession of the suit premises from the defendant. 20.Given these facts, there is no requirement for the plaintiff to go through rigmarole of a protracted trial to repossess his property. The plaintiff is entitled to decree of possession at this stage itself. The defence taken by the defendant as to the payment of Rs. 3,00,000/­ to the plaintiff as loan cannot be taken as defence to the suit for possession. 21. In view of the same, the suit is liable to be decreed for the relief Suit No.539/11 Page 10 of 11 Shyam Sunder V. Pooja Verma of possession. A decree of possession under Order XII Rule 6 of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908 is passed in favour of plaintiff and against the defendant in respect of the suit premises bearing no. B­96, Upper Ground Floor, Jawahar Park, Khanpur, Devali, New Delhi - 110062. The defendant is directed to handover the vacant possession of the suit premises within 30 days fromt he date of this order. Let decree sheet be drawn accordingly. 22. With respect to plaintiffs entitlement of mesne profits and damages, the plaintiff may lead necessary evidence on 06.11.13. Announced in the open (SHREYA ARORA MEHTA) th Court on 24 September, 2013 CIVIL JUDGE­1 (SOUTH DISTRICT) (Judgment contains 11 pages) SAKET COURTS, NEW DELHI Suit No.539/11 Page 11 of 11
